item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Image has been replaced.

New image is NYC Transit 100, by Ken, under CC By 2.0.

Image has been edited to crop out the back, remove the bars, as well as the sign.

item.title: Re: Who wrote this?
item.content:encoded: As a general note, you can usually check authorship of a page via the "History" link at the bottom of the main article (not the discussion page). Usually the author of iteration 0 (the posting of the page) is the author.

item.title: Who wrote this?
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user has been permanently banned for sockpuppeting and ban evasion.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image was originally posted to flickr and has since been taken down. As such, it's license cannot be verified and the image has since been taken down, under the assumption that it is not compliant with CC 3.0.

item.title: I've been reading every SCP from 2-present day, and I'm here so far.
item.content:encoded: Maybe we could get some updated passengers with some modern recovery dates and new stories? Would be cool to see.

item.title: Should SCP-4163 be crosslinked in this?
item.content:encoded: I feel like it should since they’re the only two articles that mention a “SCP Federation”, with the latter detailing how the Federation came to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the years of previous passengers be redacted to add a little shock?

item.title: That Jack Bright link though
item.content:encoded: Holy crap. One minute you think you are reading about a neat SCP that takes like 5 minutes of your time and the next you click a link to a massive story that basically serves as its own novel. I love the effort to make something seemingly simple part of a more complex web of connections and references. You really can choose if you want to take the face value enjoyment of the main SCP article or make the deep dive plunge into a bigger lore with one click of a hyperlink.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show 
- Hide

To link to a tale featuring a mentioned character, I propose the following edit:

That he worked for Dr. Jack Bright.

to

That he worked for Dr. Jack Bright.

item.title: Re: Is 59th St. and 8th Avenue station a real station?
item.content:encoded: I thought the nearest was the 59th street columbus circle station on the 8th ave line

item.title: Re: Is 59th St. and 8th Avenue station a real station?
item.content:encoded: That's what they WANT you to think!

item.title: Re: Is 59th St. and 8th Avenue station a real station?
item.content:encoded:
No, it's not a real station.

The nearest is the 8th avenue subway.

item.title: Is 59th St. and 8th Avenue station a real station?
item.content:encoded:
Is 59th St. and 8th Avenue station a real station

should it be data expunged?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this one's incredibly interesting, personally. the people coming from the future implies things, it's a fairly intellectual article

item.title: Permission to edit the passenger log now that 2012 has passed?
item.content:encoded:
It immediately struck me that the foundation would give the passenger who was sent back 4 years amnestics and set her loose again once her "present" instance had boarded the train. While I realise they're keeping people from entering it, the risk of temporal anomalies that comes with keeping duplicates of a person around would be worth making an exception for this passenger. It would also be a valuable oppurtunity for study if they used the passenger as an attempt to send a simple note back in time.

The note is probably a bit much, but I think the resolving of one of the time travel passengers situations would be worth doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well its a good idea, but the inconsistencies between what time or "what dimension" a person boarding the train will end up in kind of leaves me with a bit of an empty feeling, though I don't think it calls for any revision or rewriting; just an opinion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's very good article. Although it's just time travelling train it adds some creepiness that all passengers have not been found. One thing still makes me think…
About one passenger:
"She has been determined to currently exist in this timeline and must be held indefinitely to prevent unwanted temporal effects"

If she went back in time 4 years. When the "original one" in this timeline gets four years forward does that mean there will be 3 of them when no.2 comes from train, there is already one held by facility. Although if the person has clone in this timeline. Doesn't it mean that the person should have one clone before the person?
Therefore it should create Infinite number of clones appearing as 4 years younger than the previous one? This is what is mysterious. Where did all the others go? +1 for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems to me that 052-6 goes against the theme of the article. We're not told anything about the future or the nature of the train up until that point; suddenly, we're given a bunch of information about things that are mostly irrelevant to the article. I really enjoyed this, otherwise; still more than deserving of a +1. Just a minor quibble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Needs more cowbell…

Actually, no, it needs more clinicality. I think that's what it needs, anyway. I'm not digging it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"the latter claims he thought SCP-052 was a 300th Anniversary Special train."

He who? Was there a sentence that came before this one that got edited out?

item.title: Note to image team
item.content:encoded: I was trying to figure out where I got the image for this, and can't find it via Google or tineye. There are a few similar ones (it's not a vintage photo, but of a restored train that they run on special occasions) but I don't see this exact image.

item.title: Re: +101
item.content:encoded:
Neat concept + solid writing = damn good article. Good work eric_h, keep it up.

Something I wanted to mention, since none of the comments above did, and I missed it on the first read-through:

Passenger 052-6 isn't merely working for Bright. He is Level 4 Supervisor. In the future, Bright is O5. Well, I guess it isn't such a huge surprise, but nevertheless.

Love the other two cross-tests too. This is how cross-testing should be done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, "Demon Horse." I guess it is possible that with its adaptation abilities, it could evolve over a few decades and no longer be a reptile.

And yes, I'm aware of how old that post is. I just wanted to say that.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: For all its flaws, this was one of the very first SCPs I read, and one of the ones that got me hooked on the site. Congrats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not going to lie, at first I was uninterested in this article, seemed fairly mundane and easy enough to contain from every being a problem again. Then I read the "Log of Recovered Passengers" and was immediately brought to attention. Do wish these went on a bit more as it mentions there are 26 people in custody currently. Anyways, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, but I find that the dismembering and, most importantly, the "send no more" note to be detracting from this SCP.

Anyway, +1. I always liked the idea of time-traveling trains, and this one grabs my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one has been bugging the hell out of me since I first read it a few months ago and I finally figured out why.

Passenger 052-6 came back from the future to save Passenger 052-4 from going through whatever it was that he went through. Why? The Foundation doesn't like paradoxes, which this could easily create, so unless this Agent shits diamonds I don't see why they'd bother. And then I got it. Passenger 052-6 works for Bright in the future. What if the whole thing was just a way of making sure Bright hangs around for a few thousand more years? He's brilliant and dedicated, a combination that's not as easy to come by as some might think. Consider the following:

Bright: We've come up with a sure fire plan to release me from 963.
05: No.
Bright: ….no?
05: Did you read the report on 052? A guy we recovered claims he's from 1000 years in our future and works for YOU. Letting you attempt to get free from your mortality before AT LEAST that year could put us in BL-class predestination paradox territory. So, no.

Boom, we're promised to have a brilliant, experienced, loyal mind on tap for another 1000 years.

item.title: Passenger 052-1:
item.content:encoded:
Has any research been done to see if she is from an alternate reality? If not, I suggest amnesiacs to erase the last four years and dropping her off at her intended destination station at the appropriate time. Followed by limited surveillance over the next month, or so.

After that, we consider the matter closed. Maybe even get a commendation in our folders for freeing up a little bit of Foundation money budgeted for log-term non-D Class?

Think fast, though, it happens tomorrow night!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aggressively dismantled, I would imagine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can we send a robot with a video camera in, programmed to safely store the video in a known location when it departs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep in mind, articles are a snapshot in time, and not always current. We don't need to change them, just because the corresponding date has passed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, we already know she's going to get on the train… but it would make for a nice tale to have a big fight break out over trying to stop her from getting on the train at the time she reported to us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Passenger 052-1's arrival date is coming up in a few months; is the Foundation taking any action to ensure timeline stability?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfectly clear. I needed to reread the entire section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cleaned up the wording a bit and now (I think) the dates make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah! See, I didn't get the "They come back different" just that some some were from alternate time lines

Um, and you put 1976 in the wrong spot.

(Never mind that last line!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right about the dates; I unblocked and fixed those.

Why Albanian? Well, why not? The folks who ride the train come back a little bit different, in ways that don't necessarily make sense to the rest of us. The Foundation would like to figure out who is running the train and what it wants with the people taken (and why some are sent back and others not), but I think all of that works better when left as a mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One point:

Passengers leaving SCP-052 claim to have boarded on various dates, from ████, up to 2204. Subjects retain no knowledge of time on board.

"Site-21 currently holds 26 passengers from dates ranging from 2011 to 22██; the latter claims he thought SCP-052 was a 300th Anniversary Special train.

Unless someone got on had already traveled back to 2204 from a later time, I don't see the point in blocking out the year in the second sentence. And unless the Foundation is actively time traveling, I don't see the point in blocking out the early date.

You're also holding someone from 1976 making the 2011 date incorrect.

I'm also unclear why that one tourist speaks Albanian now.

item.title: Re: Bit of NYC history
item.content:encoded: I wanted to to make a similar comment, but you have summed up my feelings very well. On the other hand, I feel that an abandoned station would not have the same sense of danger to the public associated with it. This could explain why the MTA performs maintenance operations at the strangest of times.

item.title: Bit of NYC history
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if this is super relevant, but might make an interesting note:

The 59th street station has an abandoned platform right in the middle which has been disused since the early 80s. Its only used now for special vintage train trains that give special tours throughout the subway. The R1-R9s from the New York Transit Museum are functional and are used to tow cars that are unable to operate under their own power through the system; incidently enough, if you hang around the 59th Street Station long enough, you can see these vintage cars coming through.

Unfortunately, due to its location, I don't think you can feasibly seal off this station without causing massive disruptions in the NY Subway without being noticed. It might be worth changing this to use the abandoned 42nd Street (A/C/E) lower level, or possibly the abandoned City Hall (R/N) lower level.

Then again, I might be nitpicking since I'm a rainfan. I did enjoy the SCP and the "feel" of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "SCP 682? The demon horse? Yeah, we still have that one. Never could figure out how to kill it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This conversation belongs in the discussion page for 008. Take it there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, to me this is a case of "look how awesome that thing is, and I'm mentioning it here." Not a case of trying to outdo another SCP, but riding on its notoriety I think. If the reference was removed, nothing about this article would change. It would be just as cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If they could, 008 wouldn't be Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't the Foundation be able to easily deal with an 008 outbreak?
The GOC could do it if the Foundation messed up.
EDIT:
"Easily" as in only a few thousand casualties and minor, censored media coverage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. Everyone wanted to match their humanoid with 808, or be better then 076, or cross test their product to see what it would do to 682… It got boring. This? looks fine to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Erku sums up my feeling nicely, here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting point, Clef.

I submitted a downvote because of the crosslinks. The idea itself I'm not opposed to, but we don't need specific examples.

Not to mention the 'messages from the future' have been done in other articles, including one of mine. SCP-130

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/scp-342

hm.

item.title: Further thoughts...
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking about 052 (while riding the A train to work, of course) and wondered if maybe the Foundation has made a mistake by releasing some of the train passengers. We know there is an entity controlling the train, and that it is at least somewhat malevolent. Would it just send people back for no particular reason? Or has something happened to them that we can't detect, that furthers the entity's purpose?

Maybe one of the "unharmed" people could suddenly [DATA EXPUNGED] and the foundation realizes it has to not only hold everyone who has been on the train, but collect all the folks it has let go in the past.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I really think this is one of those sentiments that is getting out of hand in terms of "that's cliche". It makes sense in the context of this article, and clearly adds to the story. It shouldn't be taboo to make the Foundation Wiki cross reference itself, when it improves an SCP.

As best I can tell it became taboo because too many articles were referring to a small handful of popular SCP. That is somewhat the case with 008 in this one (personally I still think it's fine), but 699 in particular is a good, well placed reference that's not simple name-dropping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like it when articles make mention of other SCPs; it makes the whole site feel more like a complete whole, and provides intrigue for those familiar with the Foundation.

Granted, I would definitely agree that it shouldn't be a main part of the article, just something on the side (for the sake of new comers).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know cross-referencing has become a cliche, but there are some articles where it makes sense in context and doesn't feel tacked-on.

I thought it made sense to include the "Agent From the Future," given what the SCP does and the Foundation's interest in investigating it. Once you have him, it's pretty obvious that he would have a lot of useful info about our SCPs, and the Foundation would want to get their hands on it — even if some of it might well be incorrect. Maybe I could have picked better/different SCP examples, but I feel like the cross-referencing flows from the logic of the article, as opposed to just being fluff. [Except for the Dr. Bright reference — that's fluff, although I think it could be cool if Dr. Bright confronts someone who is going to be working for him 100 years from now.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP Federation agent comes from a timeline where SCP-699 was sent back from 24XX; even if it's not our timeline, they have a 699 where he came from. But of course, it might be a little different from ours…there's no way to know for sure unless ours gets opened, which is probably a very bad idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, in this instance I feel that the SCP-699 reference is appropriate. The 008 bit, on the other hand, bothers me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: References to other SCPs is one of those "argh, stop doing that we're sick to death of it on the whole" kind of things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But this refrence feels right to me. Someone figured out in his timeline how to open 699. Then all hell broke loose. He boarded that train. And told us not to open it. It sounds good to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the article would be greatly improved by removing the reference to 699, myself. This entry stands on its own, it doesn't need to refer to other articles.
